progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—identifiability of disentangled representations in more natural temporal data—and the opening makes a compelling case that temporal sparsity could provide useful structure beyond standard unsupervised disentanglement assumptions. The main strengths are the ambitious combination of empirical measurements from real video annotations, a theoretically motivated identifiability result, practical estimation methods, and new benchmarks designed to bridge synthetic and natural settings. However, the beginning also raises several significant concerns that should be clarified early: the link between observed sparsity in object-mask transitions and sparsity of the true latent generative factors is plausible but not fully justified, especially given the acknowledged dependencies among natural factors; the scope and assumptions of the identifiability proof are not yet clear enough to assess whether it truly applies to realistic visual data or only to an idealized setting; and the claim of moving “towards natural data” may be somewhat overstated if the empirical validation still relies heavily on controlled benchmarks and simplified mask-derived factors. The presentation is generally clear and engaging, but it is dense and occasionally conflates empirical observation, theoretical guarantee, and practical algorithmic performance, making it harder to distinguish what is proven, what is measured, and what is hypothesized. Overall, the paper appears strong and potentially impactful, but its quality will depend critically on whether the later sections rigorously connect the natural-transition statistics to the assumptions of the theory and demonstrate that the proposed prior improves disentanglement for reasons beyond benchmark-specific design choices.

comments.title: Claims about real-video generative statistics overgeneralize from sparse mask-derived marginals
comments.quote: To estimate these generative factors, we compute statistics on measured transitions of area and position for object masks from large-scale, natural, unstructured videos. ... Thus, we present the first work, to the best of our knowledge, which proposes a theoretically grounded solution that covers the statistics observed in real videos.
comments.explanation: The empirical evidence in this passage concerns a small set of observable mask-derived proxies—horizontal position, vertical position, and object size/area—not the full set of latent generative factors in natural videos, such as pose, illumination, texture, identity, articulation, occlusion, camera motion, or scene layout. The paper does acknowledge complex dependencies and focuses on sparse marginals, but the wording still risks implying that the theory covers the full transition statistics of real videos. The formal model instead assumes an idealized factorized generalized-Laplace transition prior, a Gaussian marginal at the previous time step, matched parameters, and no model misspecification. These assumptions cover sparse marginal transition behavior, not the broader dependent structure measured or present in natural video.

comments.title: Claims of minimal or no impractical assumptions understate the formal assumptions
comments.quote: We show that equipping practical estimation methods with our prior often surpasses the current state-of-the-art on several established benchmark datasets without any impractical assumptions, such as knowledge of the number of changing generative factors. ... Similar to Hyvärinen et al. ... we only assume that the latent factors are temporally dependent
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is valid: the method avoids the specific weak-supervision assumption that the number of changing factors between paired observations is known. However, phrases such as “without any impractical assumptions” and “only assume temporal dependence” are broader than the paper’s own setup supports. The theory also assumes sparse/non-Gaussian temporal innovations, factorized latent transitions, a Gaussian previous-time marginal, injective differentiable generators, correct model specification, and matched prior parameters. A more precise claim would be that the method assumes temporal dependence with sparse transition structure, while avoiding known changed-factor counts.

comments.title: Overbroad description of Khemakhem et al. generalization
comments.quote: This contribution was generalized by Khemakhem et al. (2020a) past the nonlinear ICA domain to any consistent parameter estimation method for deep latent-variable models, including the VAE framework.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is understandable: Khemakhem et al. connect nonlinear ICA identifiability ideas to latent-variable models and show that, under suitable conditional-prior/exponential-family assumptions, identifiable models can in principle be learned by consistent estimation procedures, including VAE-like approaches. However, the phrase “any consistent parameter estimation method for deep latent-variable models” reads more broadly than the standard result supports. The identifiability guarantee depends on specific auxiliary-variable conditional structure and sufficient variability/rank conditions; it is not a model-agnostic guarantee for arbitrary deep latent-variable estimators.

comments.title: Continuous Laplace-style transition prior does not literally imply unchanged factors
comments.quote: Such a prior would intuitively allow for sharp changes in some latent factors, while most other factors remain unchanged between adjacent time-points.
comments.explanation: The sparse-prior intuition is broadly correct: an L1 or Laplace innovation prior has a sharp peak at zero and heavier tails than a Gaussian, so it encourages many small innovations and occasional large ones. But under a continuous factorized Laplace or generalized-Laplace distribution, exact zero transitions have probability zero. Thus “remain unchanged” is literally stronger than what the model encodes unless an atom at zero, a thresholding rule, or a discrete transition process is added. The more accurate interpretation is that most factors change only slightly.

comments.title: Gaussian marginal with additive generalized-Laplace transitions is not a stationary temporal model, and the CLT footnote does not resolve this
comments.quote: p(z_t−1) is a factorized Gaussian prior N(0, I) ... and p(z_t|z_t−1) is a factorized generalized Laplace distribution. ... In case of an autoregressive process with non-Gaussian innovations with finite variance, it follows from the central limit theorem that the marginal distribution converges to a Gaussian in the limit of large λ.
comments.explanation: If the transition is centered on z_{t−1}, then z_t equals z_{t−1} plus a generalized-Laplace innovation. Starting from z_{t−1} ~ N(0, I), the marginal distribution of z_t is the convolution of a Gaussian and a generalized-Laplace distribution, not again N(0, I). Thus the pairwise model may be valid as an asymmetric two-time prior, but it should not be presented as a stationary temporal process with Gaussian instantaneous marginals unless an additional restoring or damping mechanism is specified. The footnote’s CLT justification is also unclear: λ has been defined as a distribution rate/precision parameter, not as the number of summed innovations or a time horizon. Increasing a rate parameter changes innovation scale; it does not by itself make the marginal Gaussian.

comments.title: Proof sketch understates the step from distribution preservation to signed permutations
comments.quote: For these distributions to be the same, the function h must preserve the Gaussian marginal for the first time step as well as the joint distribution, implying that it must preserve both the vector lengths and distances in the latent space. As we argue in the extended proof, this can only be the case if h is a composition of permutations and sign flips.
comments.explanation: The high-level intuition is sensible: a standard Gaussian marginal permits rotations, while a factorized non-Gaussian transition prior should break rotational symmetry down to coordinate permutations and sign flips. However, preserving Gaussian vector lengths and ordinary Euclidean distances would only imply an orthogonal transformation, not a signed permutation. The signed-permutation conclusion requires using the specific factorized generalized-Laplace transition structure, or equivalently preservation of the relevant non-Euclidean transition law for α ≠ 2. Since the theorem’s main contribution rests on this reduction, the proof sketch should make that nontrivial step clearer.

comments.title: Practical claim about choosing any α < 2 exceeds the exact theorem assumptions
comments.quote: For the former, any α < 2 would break the general rotation symmetry by having an optimum for axis-aligned representations, which theorem 1 includes as a requirement for identifiability.
comments.explanation: The intuition that non-Gaussian generalized-Laplace exponents below 2 break rotational symmetry is reasonable. The issue is the appeal to Theorem 1: the theorem assumes the model’s α matches the true α, i.e. no shape-parameter misspecification. The paper then notes that natural fits suggest α ≈ 0.5 but uses α = 1 in SlowVAE for tractability. That may work empirically and may still encourage axis-aligned coordinates, but it is not what the theorem formally guarantees. The claim should distinguish exact identifiability under correct specification from empirical robustness under misspecification.

comments.title: Claim of empirical improvement over previous approaches is too unconditional for mixed results
comments.quote: Despite these violations, we consider it a strength of our method that the practical implementation still achieves improved disentanglement over previous approaches.
comments.explanation: The intended point is reasonable: despite violations of the theorem’s assumptions, the method remains empirically useful. However, the statement sounds unconditional. The reported tables show a mixed picture: SlowVAE is best on some metrics and datasets, while PCL or Ada-GVAE are better on others, including several Natural Discrete metrics and some dSprites Uniform/Laplace metrics. The evidence supports a qualified claim such as “often improves” or “improves on several benchmarks/metrics,” not a blanket claim that it achieves improved disentanglement over previous approaches.

comments.title: Dataset descriptions overstate how fully natural or matched the generative transitions are
comments.quote: LAP ... matches the marginal distribution of natural transitions. ... Natural Sprites consists of pairs of rendered sprite images with generative factors sampled from real YouTube-VOS transitions. ... KITTI Masks ... with natural shapes and continuous natural transitions in all underlying factors.
comments.explanation: These dataset constructions are useful, but the wording overstates what they establish. For LAP, using Laplace-distributed changes matches the qualitative sparsity/heavy-tailed character of natural transitions, but earlier natural fits are described as generalized Laplace with α around 0.5, so it is not clear that the actual marginal distributions are matched. For Natural Sprites, only position and scale are sampled from measured YouTube-VOS transitions, while shape and orientation are fixed or simplified. For KITTI Masks, real segmentation masks contain natural position, scale, pose, silhouette, and occlusion variation, but they do not provide explicit ground truth for all latent causes such as articulated pose, depth, identity, occlusion state, or camera motion. The descriptions should distinguish measured or controlled mask-derived factors from all underlying generative factors.

comments.title: KITTI-MOTS timing values appear inconsistent with the standard dataset frame rate
comments.quote: We consider adjacent frames which correspond to mean(Δt) = 0.05s in physical time ... as well as frames with a larger temporal gap of mean(Δt) = 0.15s, which corresponds to samples of pairs that are at most 5 frames apart.
comments.explanation: KITTI tracking/KITTI-MOTS is conventionally treated as 10 Hz video, for which adjacent frames correspond to about 0.1 s, not 0.05 s. The stated 0.15 s mean for pairs at most 5 frames apart would be consistent with uniformly sampling 1–5 frame gaps only if the frame interval were 0.05 s, i.e. 20 Hz. If the authors used different timestamps, resampled videos, or a subset with different timing, that needs to be stated explicitly. As written, the numerical time scale is difficult to reconcile with the standard KITTI-MOTS frame rate and may affect reproducibility and interpretation of the reported dynamics.

comments.title: Hyperparameter and model-selection claims need clearer qualification
comments.quote: We scaled up the implementation of PCL for evaluation on our high-dimensional pixel inputs, and note this method does not have any hyperparameters. ... [SlowVAE] outperforms existing approaches ... on aggregate without any tuning of hyperparameters to individual datasets.
comments.explanation: The PCL statement may mean that PCL lacks a disentanglement-specific regularization coefficient, but as written it is too broad: implementations still require choices such as architecture, representation dimension, optimizer, learning rate, batch size, negative/permuted sample construction, and training duration. Similarly, the claim that SlowVAE uses no dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning is ambiguous because the method involves α, λ, and γ, and the broader context mentions random search and unsupervised model selection. The paper should clarify whether settings were fixed globally, whether UDR or other selection was run separately per dataset, and whether no supervised tuning with ground-truth metrics was used.

comments.title: Model selection for visualizations is ambiguous on continuous datasets
comments.quote: Although we train on image pairs, our model does not need paired data points at test time. For all visualizations, we pick the models with the highest average score across the DisLib metrics.
comments.explanation: The paper explains that DisLib metrics do not support the continuous Natural Sprites and KITTI Masks evaluations, yet later includes visualizations for those settings. If “all visualizations” includes continuous datasets, it is unclear what score was used to select the visualized model. If MCC or another metric was used instead, that should be stated. The issue is mainly ambiguity about the model-selection rule outside the discrete DisLib setting.

comments.title: Claim of statistical significance is not supported by the reported information
comments.quote: Namely, the increase in performance for larger time gap is more pronounced with SlowVAE than the baselines, resulting in a statistically significant MCC gain.
comments.explanation: The reported means and standard deviations suggest that SlowVAE improves more at the larger temporal gap, but the passage does not specify what hypothesis test was run. It is also unclear whether the claimed significant gain refers to SlowVAE versus its own shorter-gap result, SlowVAE versus baselines at the larger gap, or an interaction comparing gap-dependent improvements across models. With 10 seeds, means and standard deviations alone are not enough to establish statistical significance; a test statistic, p-value, confidence interval, or clear testing protocol is needed.

comments.title: MCC terminology and categorical/angular evaluation discussion are underspecified
comments.quote: MCC ... measures rank correlation between the matching latent factor ... Figure 5: ... absolute MCC between generative and model factors ... One solution is to modify MCC to report the maximum correlation over all permutations of the ground truth assignments ... This could also explain why all models considered ... perform comparably on the Natural Sprites datasets
comments.explanation: Several related points about MCC need clarification. In the ICA literature, MCC usually denotes a scalar mean absolute correlation coefficient after optimal assignment, commonly using Pearson correlation unless otherwise specified; “rank correlation” would instead suggest Spearman correlation. The displayed matrices also appear to be sorted absolute pairwise correlation matrices, not MCC itself, so the caption should distinguish the matrix from the scalar MCC summary. Finally, the proposed fix for categorical labels—maximizing correlation over permutations of label assignments—would address arbitrary ordinal ordering only partially and could still be inadequate for unordered categorical or circular angular factors. The speculation that categorical/angular factors explain comparable model performance is plausible, but it needs more evidence about factor encodings and each factor’s contribution to the reported metrics.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—identifiability of disentangled representations in more natural temporal data—and the opening makes a compelling case that temporal sparsity could provide useful structure beyond standard unsupervised disentanglement assumptions. The main strengths are the ambitious combination of empirical measurements from real video annotations, a theoretically motivated identifiability result, practical estimation methods, and new benchmarks designed to bridge synthetic and natural settings. However, the beginning also raises several significant concerns that should be clarified early: the link between observed sparsity in object-mask transitions and sparsity of the true latent generative factors is plausible but not fully justified, especially given the acknowledged dependencies among natural factors; the scope and assumptions of the identifiability proof are not yet clear enough to assess whether it truly applies to realistic visual data or only to an idealized setting; and the claim of moving “towards natural data” may be somewhat overstated if the empirical validation still relies heavily on controlled benchmarks and simplified mask-derived factors. The presentation is generally clear and engaging, but it is dense and occasionally conflates empirical observation, theoretical guarantee, and practical algorithmic performance, making it harder to distinguish what is proven, what is measured, and what is hypothesized. Overall, the paper appears strong and potentially impactful, but its quality will depend critically on whether the later sections rigorously connect the natural-transition statistics to the assumptions of the theory and demonstrate that the proposed prior improves disentanglement for reasons beyond benchmark-specific design choices.

comments.title: Evidence for sparse transitions is based on a limited proxy for generative factors
comments.quote: To estimate these generative factors, we compute statistics on measured transitions of area and position for object masks from large-scale, natural, unstructured videos.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: they use object-mask position and area as observable, physically meaningful factors whose temporal changes can be measured in natural videos, and the following sentence correctly specifies that the measurements are of area and position. That context partially resolves the concern. What remains problematic is the phrase “these generative factors,” which could be read as saying that the paper estimates the underlying generative factors of natural scenes in general. In fact, the evidence in this passage concerns a small set of mask-derived proxies—horizontal position, vertical position, and object size/area—not the full set of latent generative factors in natural videos, such as pose, illumination, texture, identity, articulation, occlusion, camera motion, or scene layout. The later caveat that the study focuses on sparse marginals helps, but the wording still slightly overgeneralizes from measured mask statistics to natural generative factors broadly.

comments.title: Claim that the theory covers real-video statistics overstates what the stated model handles
comments.quote: Thus, we present the first work, to the best of our knowledge, which proposes a theoretically grounded solution that covers the statistics observed in real videos.
comments.explanation: The authors are careful immediately before this to acknowledge that natural factors exhibit “complex dependencies” and that the present study focuses on sparse marginals. That is an important qualification, and it makes clear what aspect of the observed data motivates the theory. However, the quoted sentence then says the solution “covers the statistics observed in real videos,” which is stronger than what the later formal model appears to establish. In Section 3, the identifiable model assumes a factorized generalized Laplace transition prior, a Gaussian marginal at the previous time step, matched parameters, and no model misspecification. Those assumptions cover sparse marginal transition behavior, but not the complex dependencies between factors that the passage itself notes are present in natural videos. A careful reader might therefore think the identifiability result applies to the full empirical transition structure measured in natural videos, whereas it applies to an idealized subset of those statistics.

comments.title: “Without any impractical assumptions” is too broad relative to the theoretical assumptions
comments.quote: We show that equipping practical estimation methods with our prior often surpasses the current state-of-the-art on several established benchmark datasets without any impractical assumptions, such as knowledge of the number of changing generative factors.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with methods that assume knowledge of the number of changing factors is clear, and avoiding that particular assumption is a legitimate advantage. The phrase “such as” also signals an example rather than an exhaustive list. Still, “without any impractical assumptions” is broader than the surrounding theory and experimental setup support. The formal identifiability result later requires, among other things, an injective differentiable generator, correct model specification, matched transition parameters \(\lambda\) and \(\alpha\), factorized latent transitions, and sparse non-Gaussian innovations. Some of these are standard for identifiability theory, but they are still strong assumptions and not obviously practical in natural data. A more precise claim would be that the method avoids the specific impractical assumption of knowing how many factors change between paired observations, rather than implying the absence of impractical assumptions altogether.

comments.title: Overbroad description of Khemakhem et al. generalization
comments.quote: This contribution was generalized by Khemakhem et al. (2020a) past the nonlinear ICA domain to any consistent parameter estimation method for deep latent-variable models, including the VAE framework.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: Khemakhem et al. connect nonlinear ICA identifiability ideas to latent-variable models and show that, under suitable conditional-prior/exponential-family assumptions, identifiable models can in principle be learned by consistent estimation procedures, including VAE-like maximum-likelihood/variational approaches. However, the phrase "any consistent parameter estimation method for deep latent-variable models" reads more broadly than the standard result supports. The identifiability does not apply to arbitrary deep latent-variable models or arbitrary priors; it depends on specific auxiliary-variable conditional structure and sufficient variability/rank conditions. Context later makes clear that the present paper is concerned with particular temporal priors, so this is not fatal, but the wording could mislead readers into thinking Khemakhem et al. established a model-agnostic guarantee for all consistent deep latent-variable estimators.

comments.title: “Only assume temporal dependence” understates additional assumptions
comments.quote: Similar to Hyvärinen et al. (Hyvärinen and Morioka, 2016; Hyvärinen et al., 2018), we only assume that the latent factors are temporally dependent, thus avoiding assuming knowledge of the number of factors where the two observations differ (Shu et al., 2019; Locatello et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The intended point is reasonable: unlike methods that construct pairs with a known number of changed factors, the proposed approach does not require that particular weak-supervision signal. The surrounding paragraph also makes clear that the authors use an \(L_1\)-sparse temporal prior, so a careful reader can infer that temporal dependence is not the whole story. Still, the word "only" is too strong relative to the paper's own formal setup. The theory later assumes, among other things, sparse/non-Gaussian temporal innovations, factorized latent transitions, a Gaussian marginal at the previous time step, injective differentiable generators, and matched prior parameters. Even for the practical method, the temporal dependence is specifically modeled through a sparse prior, not merely through arbitrary dependence over time. A more precise statement would be that they assume temporal dependence with sparse transition structure, but do not assume known changed-factor counts.

comments.title: Continuous \(L_1\)/Laplace prior does not literally imply unchanged factors
comments.quote: Such a prior would intuitively allow for sharp changes in some latent factors, while most other factors remain unchanged between adjacent time-points.
comments.explanation: The intuition is aligned with sparse coding: an \(L_1\) or Laplace innovation prior has a sharp peak at zero and heavier tails than a Gaussian, so it encourages many small innovations and occasional large ones. However, if the prior is a continuous factorized Laplace/generalized Laplace distribution, then exact zero transitions have probability zero. Thus, "remain unchanged" is literally stronger than what the model encodes unless an additional atom at zero, thresholding rule, or discrete transition process is introduced. The broader context partly resolves this because the paper often describes transitions as "typically small" with occasional jumps, which is the more accurate interpretation. Still, given the nearby comparison to methods that assume a known number of changing factors, the wording could mislead readers into thinking the proposed prior models exact unchanged coordinates.

comments.title: Gaussian slowness corresponds to squared \(L_2\), not the Euclidean distance itself
comments.quote: Almost all similar methods are variants of slow feature analysis (SFA, Wiskott and Sejnowski, 2002), which measure slowness in terms of the Euclidean (i.e. _L_ 2, or log Gaussian) distance between temporally adjacent encodings.
comments.explanation: The intended comparison between Gaussian/SFA-style slowness and the authors' sparse \(L_1\)-style temporal prior is clear. But technically, a Gaussian transition model yields a negative log-density proportional to the squared Euclidean norm, \(\|z_t-z_{t-1}\|_2^2\), up to constants, not to the Euclidean norm \(\|z_t-z_{t-1}\|_2\) itself. Classical SFA also minimizes expected squared temporal differences under normalization constraints. This distinction is usually minor because both are commonly grouped as \(L_2\)-type penalties, but the phrase "Euclidean (i.e. \(L_2\), or log Gaussian) distance" conflates distance, squared distance, and log-probability. For a paper contrasting Laplace/\(L_1\) and Gaussian/\(L_2\) temporal priors, the precise statement should be "squared \(L_2\)" or "negative log Gaussian transition penalty."

comments.title: Gaussian marginal and additive Laplace transition are not a stationary temporal model
comments.quote: where _λ_ is the distribution rate, _p_ ( **z** _t−_ 1) is a factorized Gaussian prior _N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** ) (as in Kingma and Welling, 2013) and _p_ ( **z** _t|_ **z** _t−_ 1) is a factorized generalized Laplace distribution
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a local pairwise prior rather than a full stochastic process, in which case the asymmetry between the first and second time step may be intentional. However, the surrounding text repeatedly motivates the model as a temporal generative process for adjacent video frames, and the footnote explicitly discusses stationarity. If the conditional generalized Laplace is centered on **z**_{t-1}, as the later discussion of temporal transitions and the SlowVAE prior implies, then **z**_t = **z**_{t-1} plus generalized-Laplace innovation. Starting from **z**_{t-1} \sim N(0,I), the marginal of **z**_t is the convolution of a Gaussian and a generalized Laplace distribution, not again N(0,I). Thus this is not a stationary process with Gaussian instantaneous marginals unless an additional restoring/damping term or state-dependent transition is specified. The theorem can still be a valid statement about an asymmetric pair distribution, but the text risks misleading the reader into thinking this is a coherent stationary temporal prior for videos.

comments.title: CLT justification in the footnote appears to misuse the rate parameter
comments.quote: In case of an autoregressive process with non-Gaussian innovations with finite variance, it follows from the central limit theorem that the marginal distribution converges to a Gaussian in the limit of large _λ_ .
comments.explanation: I tried to interpret this charitably as an appeal to the usual fact that sums of many independent finite-variance innovations can become approximately Gaussian. But in the passage _λ_ has just been defined as the distribution rate of the generalized Laplace transition, not as the number of summed innovations or a time horizon. Increasing a rate/precision parameter changes the scale of each innovation; by itself it does not invoke a central-limit limit, and without normalization it would typically shrink the innovation variance rather than Gaussianize the marginal. For a linear AR process, Gaussian approximation of the stationary marginal would require additional conditions, such as many weakly weighted independent innovation terms; it is not a direct consequence of simply taking the generalized-Laplace rate large. If _λ_ is an OCR error for some time or mixing parameter, the statement should be clarified, because as written it conflicts with the earlier definition of _λ_.

comments.title: Proof sketch understates the nontrivial step from distribution preservation to signed permutations
comments.quote: For these distributions to be the same, the function _h_ must preserve the Gaussian marginal for the first time step as well as the joint distribution, implying that it must preserve both the vector lengths and distances in the latent space. As we argue in the extended proof, this can only be the case if _h_ is a composition of permutations and sign flips.
comments.explanation: The high-level intuition is sensible: a standard Gaussian marginal permits rotations, while a factorized non-Gaussian transition prior should break rotational symmetry down to coordinate permutations and sign flips. However, the wording here skips a mathematically delicate point. Preserving Gaussian vector lengths and ordinary Euclidean distances would only imply an orthogonal transformation, not a signed permutation. The signed-permutation conclusion requires using the specific factorized generalized-Laplace transition structure, equivalently preservation of the relevant \(\ell_\alpha\)-type transition law for \(\alpha \ne 2\), not merely preservation of generic distances. Since the theorem’s main contribution rests on this reduction, the proof sketch as written could lead a careful reader to an incorrect intermediate conclusion unless the appendix supplies the missing argument in full.

comments.title: Practical claim about choosing any \(\alpha<2\) is stronger than the stated theorem
comments.quote: For the former, any _α <_ 2 would break the general rotation symmetry by having an optimum for axis-aligned representations, which theorem 1 includes as a requirement for identifiability.
comments.explanation: The intuition that any non-Gaussian sparse generalized-Laplace exponent below 2 breaks the rotational symmetry is reasonable. What concerned me is the appeal to Theorem 1: the theorem explicitly assumes _α[∗]_ = _α <_ 2, i.e. no misspecification of the shape parameter. In the very next sentences the authors note that natural fits suggest _α ≈ 0.5_ but use _α = 1_ in SlowVAE for tractability. That may still work empirically and may still prefer axis-aligned coordinates, but it is not what the theorem formally guarantees. The remaining issue is a mild overextension from exact identifiability under a correctly specified transition family to robustness under shape-parameter misspecification.

comments.title: Overbroad claim of empirical improvement despite mixed results
comments.quote: Despite these violations, we consider it a strength of our method that the practical implementation still achieves improved disentanglement over previous approaches.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent here: after listing several ways in which the theorem's assumptions fail in practice, they want to emphasize that the method remains empirically useful. That is a reasonable and important point, and elsewhere the paper uses more cautious language such as “often surpasses.” However, the statement as written sounds unconditional. The immediately following table shows a more mixed picture: SlowVAE is best on some metrics/datasets, but PCL or Ada-GVAE are better on others, including several metrics for Natural Discrete and some metrics for dSprites Uniform/Laplace. Thus the evidence supports a qualified claim such as “often improves” or “improves on several benchmarks/metrics,” but not the blanket assertion that it achieves improved disentanglement over previous approaches.

comments.title: Laplace-transition dataset is said to match natural marginals more strongly than justified
comments.quote: LAP is a step towards natural data that closely resembles previous extensions of DisLib datasets to the time domain, but in a way that matches the marginal distribution of natural transitions (see Appendix D.2 for more details).
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear: replacing uniformly sampled factor changes with sparse, nearby changes is much closer to the empirical transition histograms in Fig. 1. That is a useful dataset construction. The concern is with the word “matches.” Earlier, the measured natural transitions are described as generalized Laplace with fitted shape values around \(\alpha \approx 0.5\), while the LAP procedure here is described as using ordinary Laplace-distributed probabilities. Unless Appendix D.2 fits the scale and possibly shape separately for each factor and accounts for discretization/truncation, this construction matches the qualitative sparsity/heavy-tailed character, not necessarily the actual marginal distribution of natural transitions. A reader could reasonably infer a closer statistical match than has been established in the passage.

comments.title: Natural Sprites wording suggests all factor transitions are natural, but only some are measured from video
comments.quote: **(2) Natural Sprites** consists of pairs of rendered sprite images with generative factors sampled from real YouTube-VOS transitions.
comments.explanation: The subsequent sentences partially resolve the concern by explaining that position and scale are set from measured adjacent YouTube-VOS values, while shapes and orientations are simple and fixed for a given pair. That clarification is helpful. Still, the quoted sentence is broader than the actual construction: not all generative factors of the rendered sprites are sampled from real YouTube-VOS transitions. Shape is fixed because of object permanence, and orientation is fixed because it is hard to estimate reliably from masks. The issue is not the design choice, which is defensible, but the wording: it could lead readers to think the full generative-factor transition vector is natural rather than only the measured position/scale components.

comments.title: KITTI-MOTS timing values appear inconsistent with the standard dataset frame rate
comments.quote: We consider adjacent frames which correspond to mean(∆ _t_ ) = 0 _._ 05 _s_ in physical time (we report the mean because of variable sampling rates in the original data); as well as frames with a larger temporal gap of mean(∆ _t_ ) = 0 _._ 15 _s_ , which corresponds to samples of pairs that are at most 5 frames apart.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to specify the physical time scale of the KITTI Masks transitions, which is important because transition sparsity depends strongly on the temporal gap. However, KITTI tracking/KITTI-MOTS is conventionally treated as 10 Hz video, for which adjacent frames correspond to about 0.1 s, not 0.05 s. The stated 0.15 s mean for pairs at most 5 frames apart would be consistent with uniformly sampling 1–5 frame gaps only if the frame interval were 0.05 s, i.e. 20 Hz. If the authors used a different source timing, resampled videos, or a subset with different timestamps, that needs to be stated explicitly. As written, the numerical time scale is hard to reconcile with the standard KITTI-MOTS frame rate and could affect reproducibility and interpretation of the reported natural dynamics.

comments.title: “All underlying factors” in KITTI Masks overstates what segmentation masks make controlled or observable
comments.quote: **(3) KITTI Masks** is composed of pedestrian segmentation masks from the autonomous driving vision benchmark KITTI-MOTS, thus with natural shapes and continuous natural transitions in all underlying factors.
comments.explanation: The construction has a real advantage: unlike rendered sprites, the masks come from real video and therefore contain natural changes in position, scale, silhouette, pose, and occlusion. But the phrase “all underlying factors” is stronger than what the dataset can support. Pedestrian masks do not provide explicit ground truth for all latent causes of appearance, such as articulated pose, occlusion state, depth, camera motion, identity, or category-level properties; later the passage even notes that object categories never change across transitions. If “underlying factors” means only the measured mask-derived factors used for evaluation, that should be stated. Otherwise the wording risks implying a fully natural and fully observed latent-factor process, which is not established.

comments.title: PCL is described as having no hyperparameters despite nontrivial implementation choices
comments.quote: We scaled up the implementation of PCL for evaluation on our high-dimensional pixel inputs, and note this method does not have any hyperparameters.
comments.explanation: I first read this as an attempt to distinguish PCL from VAE-style methods that require tuning a regularization coefficient such as β or γ. In that narrower sense, the authors may mean that PCL has no disentanglement-specific regularization hyperparameter to select. However, the statement as written is stronger: PCL implementations still require choices such as encoder architecture, representation dimensionality, optimizer, learning rate, batch size, number/construction of negative or permuted samples, training duration, and in practice possibly temporal-window or segment choices. The preceding clause also says the authors “scaled up” PCL to high-dimensional pixel inputs, which is itself a nontrivial methodological modification. Unless all of these choices are fixed exactly by the cited PCL method or fully specified elsewhere, saying the method has no hyperparameters is misleading and weakens the fairness/reproducibility of the comparison.

comments.title: Model selection for visualizations is inconsistent with continuous datasets
comments.quote: Although we train on image pairs, our model does not need paired data points at test time. For all visualizations, we pick the models with the highest average score across the DisLib metrics.
comments.explanation: The authors have just explained that DisLib metrics do not support the continuous Natural Sprites and KITTI Masks evaluations, and the passage later includes a KITTI Masks visualization in Fig. 4. That made me check whether “all visualizations” could mean only DisLib visualizations, but the surrounding text does not say so. If this rule also applies to KITTI or continuous Natural Sprites, it is unclear what score was actually used, since the DisLib metrics are unavailable there. If instead MCC or another metric was used for continuous datasets, that should be stated. There is also a milder concern that selecting qualitative visualizations by supervised disentanglement metrics can produce optimistic-looking examples, although the authors do disclose the selection rule. The main remaining problem is ambiguity about how visualized models were selected outside the discrete DisLib setting.

comments.title: Claim of statistical significance is not supported by the reported information
comments.quote: Namely, the increase in performance for larger time gap is more pronounced with SlowVAE than the baselines, resulting in a statistically significant MCC gain.
comments.explanation: The reported means and standard deviations do suggest that SlowVAE improves more when moving from mean(∆_t_) = 0.05s to 0.15s, and the absolute difference at the larger gap is substantial. However, the passage does not specify what hypothesis test was run, whether seeds are paired across conditions, whether the claimed significant gain refers to SlowVAE versus its own shorter-gap result, SlowVAE versus Ada-GVAE/PCL at the larger gap, or an interaction effect comparing gap-dependent improvements across models. With 10 seeds, standard deviations alone are not enough to establish statistical significance under normal reporting conventions; a test statistic, p-value, confidence interval, or at least a clear testing protocol is needed. The underlying claim may be true, but it is under-justified as written.

comments.title: MCC is described as rank correlation, which may conflict with the standard ICA metric
comments.quote: This directly impacts MCC performance (third row in the MCC matrix), which measures rank correlation between the matching latent factor (an angular variable) and the ground truth, which encodes the angles with monotonically increasing indices.
comments.explanation: The discussion of angular variables and rotational symmetries is useful, and the broader point is valid: any monotonic-correlation-style metric can behave poorly for periodic factors represented by linearly increasing labels. The specific metric description is potentially inaccurate, though. In the ICA literature, MCC usually denotes a mean absolute correlation coefficient after optimal assignment, commonly using Pearson correlation unless otherwise specified. “Rank correlation” would instead suggest Spearman correlation, which has different invariances and different behavior on nonlinear but monotone relations. If the implementation indeed uses Spearman/rank correlation, that should be stated explicitly when MCC is introduced; if it uses the standard Pearson-style MCC, this sentence misdefines the metric. The issue is localized, but it can mislead readers interpreting the reported MCC matrices and scores.

comments.title: MCC is described as a matrix rather than the scalar matched-correlation score
comments.quote: Figure 5: **DSprites Latent Representations:** (Top) shows absolute MCC between generative and model factors (rows are rearranged for maximal correlation on the main diagonal).
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that each entry in the displayed matrices is an MCC value. In the ICA/nonlinear-ICA literature, however, MCC usually denotes the scalar Mean Correlation Coefficient obtained after optimally matching learned components to ground-truth sources; the entries shown here appear instead to be absolute pairwise correlations, with the scalar MCC reported above each matrix. The surrounding caption partly resolves the intent by saying that rows are rearranged for maximal correlation and that the values are percent correlation, but the phrase “absolute MCC between generative and model factors” and later “PCL MCC matrix” could mislead readers into thinking MCC itself is a matrix-valued quantity. A clearer wording would distinguish the sorted absolute correlation matrix from the scalar MCC summary.

comments.title: Proposed permutation fix for categorical MCC does not fully solve the evaluation problem
comments.quote: One solution is to modify MCC to report the maximum correlation over all permutations of the ground truth assignments, although brute force methods for this would scale poorly with the number of categories.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify a real issue: Pearson correlation with arbitrarily ordinal-encoded categories can penalize a representation that cleanly separates categories but orders them differently. I checked whether the proposed fix is only meant as a sketch, and the following sentence does acknowledge that disentanglement evaluation is an open problem. Still, maximizing correlation over permutations of category labels would only make MCC invariant to the arbitrary ordering of labels; it would not generally measure whether an unordered categorical factor is well represented. For example, a latent dimension could separate three categories in a non-monotone or multi-dimensional way, and no single numeric relabeling would make Pearson correlation an adequate statistic. Conversely, optimizing over label permutations can inflate a correlation by choosing the most favorable ordinal coding. Standard approaches would usually use classification accuracy, mutual information, or metrics designed for categorical variables rather than treating categories as ordered real values. Since this is presented only as “one solution,” the issue is localized, but the suggested modification is underspecified and potentially insufficient.

comments.title: Speculative link between number/type of categories and comparable performance is under-justified
comments.quote: We also note that datasets where we see small performance differences among models (e.g., Cars3D) have significantly more discrete categories (e.g., 183) than the other datasets (3 _−_ 6). This could also explain why all models considered in Table 1 and 2 perform comparably on the Natural Sprites datasets, where unlike KITTI Masks the ground truth evaluation includes categorical and angular variables.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: if the evaluation metric is sensitive to arbitrary ordinal encodings, then datasets with categorical or cyclic factors may obscure real representation differences. The caveat “could also explain” keeps the claim appropriately tentative. What remains unclear is the mechanism connecting the Cars3D example, which is said to have many discrete categories, to Natural Sprites, where the stated issue is the presence of categorical and angular variables rather than necessarily a large number of unordered categories. Angular variables introduce a different problem from unordered categories: their topology is circular, so linear correlation can be poor near the wrap-around even for a good representation. Without showing how many Natural Sprites factors are categorical, how they are encoded, or how much each factor contributes to the reported metrics, this explanation remains plausible but not well supported by the presented evidence.

comments.title: Claim of no dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning needs clarification
comments.quote: We apply this model to current metric-based disentanglement benchmarks to demonstrate that it outperforms existing approaches (Locatello et al., 2020; Hyvärinen and Morioka, 2017) on aggregate without any tuning of hyperparameters to individual datasets.
comments.explanation: This is an important comparison claim, so I checked it against the earlier methodological context. Earlier, the paper says that SlowVAE requires choosing \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), and \(\gamma\), and that random search can be used for \(\lambda\) and \(\gamma\); the broader context also mentions unsupervised model selection via UDR. The conclusion may mean that the final reported SlowVAE settings were fixed globally, or that no supervised tuning using ground-truth disentanglement scores was performed. But as written, “without any tuning of hyperparameters to individual datasets” could be read as saying no per-dataset model-selection procedure was used at all. If any hyperparameter search or UDR selection was performed separately by dataset, then the statement is at least ambiguous and potentially overstates the fairness of the comparison. This matters because aggregate outperformance can depend strongly on model-selection protocol in disentanglement benchmarks.
